author: Sofia Martínez
title: CRM Automation for Service Businesses: Setup Without the Consultant
description: Stop losing leads in spreadsheets. We show you how to build a working CRM automation loop in HubSpot or Pipedrive that actually converts, with zero coding required.

sections.p: We see it constantly: service businesses landing good leads, then losing them because follow-up happens whenever someone remembers. A plumber books a job for three weeks out, the lead sits idle, and by week two the prospect hired a competitor. A HVAC company quotes five jobs and manually calls each one back. A cleaning service books one-off jobs but never offers recurring plans because there's no system to ask. This costs roughly 20-30% of potential revenue sitting idle. We're going to fix that today by building a real CRM automation workflow that actually works for small teams.

sections.h2: Choose Your Platform: HubSpot Free vs. Pipedrive vs. Keap

sections.p: HubSpot Free is our default recommendation for service businesses under 10 people. You get unlimited contacts, basic automation, and a clean interface. The downside: free tier caps you at 1,000 marketing email sends per month and limited workflow triggers. Pipedrive costs $12/month per user and is built specifically for sales pipelines—you'll see your deals visually, and the interface feels less overwhelming than HubSpot. Keap (formerly Infusioncraft) is $25-49/month and bundles CRM + email + automation together, which feels simpler if you have zero tech experience but lacks depth as you scale. For this walkthrough, we're using HubSpot Free because you can follow along immediately without a credit card.

list: HubSpot Free: Best if you need email + forms + basic automation, no cost to start
Pipedrive: Best if you live in a sales pipeline view and want simplicity
Keap: Best if you want CRM + email bundled and prefer step-by-step workflows

sections.h2: Build Your Pipeline: Stages That Match Your Actual Sales Cycle

sections.p: Most service businesses fail at CRM because they copy generic pipeline stages from a template. A plumbing company doesn't need 'Negotiation' as a stage—the customer either books or they don't. We're building stages that reflect your real decision-making timeline. For a typical HVAC or cleaning service, use this: Inquiry → Qualified → Estimate Sent → Booked → Completed → Follow-up/Upsell. For a tutoring or language school, use: Inquiry → Trial Lesson Scheduled → Trial Completed → Enrolled → Active Student → At Risk → Churned. For a contracting service (roofing, electrical), use: Inquiry → Site Survey Scheduled → Survey Completed → Estimate Sent → Deal Negotiation → Contract Signed → Project Started.

The key is moving leads through stages based on actual actions, not wishful thinking. When a prospect fills out your contact form, they start at 'Inquiry.' When they book a consultation or trial, they move to stage two. When you send a quote, they move to 'Estimate Sent' and you set a reminder to follow up in 3 days. Every stage gets a clear trigger and a time limit. Most leads die at 'Estimate Sent'—that's where you need automation teeth.

sections.h2: Automate the Follow-Up Loop (The Real Revenue Driver)

sections.p: Here's the workflow we set up for every service business: When a lead reaches 'Estimate Sent,' automatically trigger an email 3 days later asking if they have questions. If they reply, manually move them to 'Negotiation' and book a call. If they don't reply in 7 days, send a second email offering a different time or approach. If they ignore that, send one final email after 14 days ('Last Check-In') positioning yourself as the helpful expert, not the desperate vendor. This three-email sequence, properly timed, recovers approximately 15-22% of otherwise-lost deals in home services. We're not automating away the relationship—we're automating the skeleton so your team only jumps in when the lead is warm.

Set up a second automation: When a deal is marked 'Booked,' automatically send a confirmation email within 2 hours with the appointment details, parking info, and what to expect. Then send a reminder 24 hours before the job. This kills the 'customer forgot about the appointment' problem and reduces no-shows by 30-40%. Then when the job is marked 'Completed,' automatically send a thank-you email with a review request link (Google, Yelp, Facebook). Include a soft upsell: 'If you're interested in monthly service, reply here or book a call.' This takes zero effort from your team and captures upsells that would otherwise vanish.

sections.quote: Most service businesses spend 6 hours per week chasing leads that are just sitting in their system waiting for follow-up. Automation doesn't replace salesmanship—it replaces busywork so your team can focus on closing.

sections.h2: Integrate Your Intake Point (Forms, Calls, Texts, Your Website)

sections.p: Your CRM is only as good as the data flowing into it. If leads come through your website form, Google Business profile, Facebook, Calendly, and text messages, they need to land in one place. In HubSpot Free, create a contact form on your website and connect your Google Business profile reviews (HubSpot integrates directly). When a lead books a call through Calendly, it syncs automatically. If you're getting calls and texts, use a tool like Twilio (cheap, under $1/month per phone number) to log those interactions into your CRM. The goal: every touchpoint feeds into one system. A lead that comes from Google should look identical to a lead from your Facebook ad—same contact record, full timeline visible.

Most service businesses lose 25-35% of leads because they arrive via three different channels and nobody consolidates them. A prospect calls your business, mentions they saw you on Google, and your admin logs them as a new contact separately from their web form inquiry three weeks prior. Now you have two contact records, the automation fires twice, and the prospect gets annoyed by duplicate emails. This is fixable with one integration decision: pick one CRM, connect your intake sources, and deduplicate monthly (HubSpot has automatic deduplication in paid plans; in Free, do it manually).
